Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe look at things when I felt like it, which wasn't often. Most weeks I'd spend maybe an hour here and there, usually late at night when I couldn't sleep because my anxiety was keeping me up. I know I should have been more organized, but I just couldn't find the energy or motivation to set up a proper schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Sometimes I wouldn't touch anything for weeks, then I'd panic when a deadline was close and try to cram everything in. It wasn't healthy, but that's just how things went for me. Other stuff in my life always seemed to take priority, or I'd just feel too down to care about studying.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting a notification that something was due soon. Sometimes a classmate would message me asking if I'd done something, and that would remind me the LMS existed. I rarely logged in just because I wanted to learn something new. It was more like damage control most of the time.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a rhythm with it. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I tried to check in more regularly, but it never stuck. The platform just felt like another thing demanding my attention when I already felt overwhelmed with everything else going on.
Q5: The discussion posts were actually kind of helpful when I participated. Talking to other students made me feel less alone, and sometimes their explanations made more sense than the lectures. The peer support aspect was the only thing that kept me somewhat connected to the course. Videos were okay too when I actually watched them.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the readings and supplementary materials. They just felt like too much effort when I was already struggling to do the bare minimum. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The optional stuff especially got pushed aside because I figured if it wasn't required, why bother when I could barely handle what was required.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if I could see an immediate benefit, like it would directly help with an assignment that was due. Or if it was interactive and I could actually do something with my hands or engage with other people. Just reading pages of text felt pointless when I was already feeling so disconnected from everything.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations I had to drag myself through, which made them feel heavy. Optional ones felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping up. It's weird because optional stuff might have helped me, but my brain just couldn't justify spending time on them when I was already behind on the mandatory work.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded some PDFs and would sometimes flip through them on my phone when I was on the bus or waiting somewhere. I also talked to classmates in person or over text about course stuff, which helped more than the actual LMS. I learn better by doing things and talking things through, not by clicking around a website.
Q10: There were times I'd sit with a classmate and we'd work through problems together for hours, but none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be thinking about course concepts while doing other things, making connections in my head. The LMS only sees clicks and timestamps, not the actual thinking and learning happening in between.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video or PDF once and then watch or read it multiple times offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab things when I could. The LMS would just show one access, but I might have reviewed that material several times on my own.
Q12: There were weeks when my anxiety got really bad and I just couldn't face anything academic. I'd feel this heaviness and sadness that made even opening my laptop feel impossible. When money stuff got tight or family things came up, school became the last thing on my mind. Those were the times I'd disappear from the course completely.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with personal stuff, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion made it worse because I'd log in, feel lost, and then just close it because I didn't have the energy to figure things out. It became a cycle of avoidance that was hard to break.
Q14: So many times. I'd scroll through materials with my eyes glazed over, not really taking anything in. I was physically there but mentally somewhere else, worrying about other things or just feeling too numb to care. The system probably logged me as active, but I wasn't really learning anything in those moments.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but I'd put it off because the anxiety of starting felt worse than the anxiety of not doing it. It's like my brain would freeze up when I thought about all the work I'd missed. By the time I finally started, I'd rush through it, which is probably why my completion rate was so low.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the course for weeks, then scramble at the last minute when something was due. It wasn't a good system, but without that pressure, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. The panic was the only thing stronger than my avoidance.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder, especially if it was encouraging. But honestly, I often didn't even look at feedback because I was afraid of what it would say. The anxiety about seeing criticism kept me from checking, which probably made things worse in the long run.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many online courses happening at once. Fifteen courses is a lot, and I couldn't keep track of what was due where. A clearer, simpler structure might have helped me stay on top of things, but instead I just felt lost in a sea of deadlines and requirements.
Q19: Having so much content spread across different modules made it hard to know where to start. The peer support intervention actually helped because it gave me real human connection, which I needed. But the overall design assumed I had more time, energy, and resources than I actually had, which made engagement harder.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a good week, where I'm clicking around trying to understand things but feeling too overwhelmed or confused to actually complete anything. Or maybe they're preparing to do work but keep getting stuck. High activity doesn't mean productive activity, especially when anxiety is involved.
Q21: That sounds like it could be me too, honestly. I managed to pass despite barely showing up in the LMS because I learned a lot through peer conversations and offline studying. When I did engage, I tried to make it count. The exam score wasn't great, but I scraped by using strategies that didn't involve the platform much.
Q22: A course log can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on, but it completely misses the emotional state behind those actions. It can't tell if someone is actually learning or just going through the motions. It also misses all the learning that happens outside the system, which for me was significant.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the course somehow. For me, it would mean showing up mentally, not just physically clicking buttons. It's about having the energy and motivation to participate, which I struggled with a lot this semester due to feeling sad and anxious most of the time.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on peer support to get through, or how my anxiety made every login feel like climbing a mountain. It wouldn't show the sadness that made me want to give up, or the offline conversations that actually taught me things. It's just numbers that don't capture what it really felt like to be me this semester.
